context_update.summary: This front matter chunk (Abstract and Introduction) of the paper 'Work hardening in Fe–Al alloys' introduces the study of work hardening in polycrystalline Fe–Al alloys with Al content up to 40 at.%. It states the key finding of a sharp maximum in work hardening rate near 26-30 at.% Al, and attributes this to a transition from single to superdislocation dipoles, predominance of straight screw dislocations, and sessile segment formation. It reviews literature data on yield stress, ductility, and work hardening rate as functions of Al content (Figs. 1-2). Two main mechanisms are cited for high work hardening in ordered states: dipole hardening and APB tube dragging. Dislocation types are linked to ordering state (DO3, B2, disordered). APB energies (Fig. 3) and elastic anisotropy (Fig. 4) are presented as influential factors. The chunk also clarifies that specimens are thermally-relaxed as-cast, coarse-grained, with no solute/precipitate effects, and that ordering state (slowly cooled) is considered. The study aims to interpret work hardening variation via dislocation structures.

Material systems: Fe–Al alloys; Fe3Al; FeAl; disordered bcc Fe-Al
Processing route: as-cast; annealed; slowly cooled
Key variables: Al content (at.%) 0 to 40; plastic strain 1-1.5%; room temperature; grain size (coarse); order state (DO3, B2)
Microstructure: dislocation dipoles (single vs superdislocation); straight screw dislocations; sessile dislocation segments; partial dislocations with Burgers vector (1/2)<111>; APB faults (APBI, APBII)
Properties: work hardening rate; yield stress; ductility; elastic stiffness constants (C11, C12, C44); Zener anisotropy factor
Mechanisms: dipole hardening; APB tube dragging; core/Peierls effects
Figures or tables: Fig. 1; Fig. 2; Fig. 3; Fig. 4

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the Experimental section and the beginning of Results (mechanical properties and dislocation structures). It details sample preparation: induction melting and drop casting into a 25 mm copper mould producing coarse grains (~0.25 mm). Binary alloys with 15, 20, 25, 28, 30, 35% Al were made, plus B-doped versions at 20 and 30% Al. Slow cooling gave B2 order in Al-rich alloys and DO3 order in 25-30% Al. Compression samples were spark-cut to 3 mm diameter or 5x5 mm square, polished, and tested at room temperature to 2-3% strain at 3e-4 s-1. Tests showed smooth plasticity with no yield points. TEM sample preparation used twin-jet electropolishing. Mechanical results confirm prior data with work hardening peak at 29% Al (range 26-29%). B addition had little effect. Dislocation structures vary with Al content: disordered alloys (15-20% Al) show single (1/2)<111> dislocations, curved with incipient cell structure and dipole formation. At 25% Al (DO3 order), loosely coupled superdislocations with APB faults appear. At 28% Al (peak hardening), superdislocations are straight near-screw, and reactions produce sessile <100> edge locks. At 30% and 35% Al, similar superdislocation structures with higher APB energy lead to closer partials and more dipole segments. APBII fringe contrast is observed in some alloys. Figures 5-19 document these structures.
processing_route: induction melting in alumina crucible under inert gas
drop casting into 25 mm diameter copper mould
room temperature compression to 2-3% strain at 3e-4 s-1
twin-jet electropolishing for TEM (25% nitric acid in methanol at -30 degrees C and 20 V)
microstructure: single dislocations with Burgers vector (1/2)<111> in disordered alloys
smoothly curved dislocations with screw preference (15-20% Al)
dipoles and small loops from trailing screw dislocations (20% Al)
loosely coupled superdislocations and APB faults (25-30% Al)
sessile <100> edge dislocations from reactions (28% Al peak)
edge dipole segments and loops (all alloys)
properties: work hardening rate peak at 26-29% Al
slight flow stress increase with B addition
mechanisms: single dislocation glide in disordered alloys
superdislocation glide with APB fault trailing
dislocation reaction locks (sessile <100> edges) blocking motion
figures_or_tables: Fig. 7 (Fe-20% Al, straight screw dislocations, edge dipoles)
Fig. 8 (Fe-25% Al, APB faults in dark field and weak beam)
Fig. 9 (Fe-25% Al, dislocation pairs and dipoles)
Fig. 11 (Fe-28% Al, dipole and non-dipole pairs)
Fig. 13 (Fe-28% Al, superdislocation reactions, weak beam)
Fig. 14 (Fe-30% Al, dislocation structures and dipoles)
Fig. 16 (Fe-30% Al, segments and APBII fringes)

context_update.summary: This chunk covers final Results (dislocation structures in Fe-35% Al) and the entire Discussion section. For Fe-35% Al, superdislocations of B2 lattice with closely spaced partials, straight near-screw superdislocations, edge dipole segments, and weak remnant DO3 fringes are documented in Figs. 17-19. The Discussion analyzes work hardening origins: flow strength peaks at 25% Al (solution/short-range-order hardening), ductility drops with Al content (with a small peak near 28% Al), and work hardening peaks near 28% Al. High hardening in ordered alloys is linked to superdislocation dipoles; no APB tubes were observed. Four-partial superdislocations were not observed. Elastic anisotropy alone cannot explain the peak; Peierls forces are suggested to make dislocations strongly screw-oriented, enhancing dipole formation. Reaction of <111> dislocations to edge <100> dislocations produces strong barriers, especially in 28-30% Al range. The small ductility peak at ~28% Al is associated with work hardening peak and constant flow stress in 26-30% Al range. The Discussion also references anomalous hardening in L1₂ alloys (Kear-Wilsdorf locks) as analogous. No new sample aliases or processing routes are introduced beyond previous ones.
microstructure: single dislocations in disordered alloys (15-20% Al)
loosely coupled superdislocations with APB faults (25% Al)
sessile <100> edge dislocations from dislocation reactions
properties: ductility (drop with Al, small peak at 28% Al)
mechanisms: dipole formation behind moving screw dislocations
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1 (flow stress and ductility vs Al)

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the final part of the Discussion (ductility peak) and the entire Summary section. It reiterates the work hardening peak near Fe-28% Al, which coincides with a fall in flow stress above 25% Al and a small ductility maximum at 28% Al due to the Considere criterion and high work hardening rate. Dislocation structures transition from single bowed dislocations in disordered materials (15-20% Al) to paired superdislocations with straight screw segments in highly ordered materials (28-35% Al). DO3 ordered Fe-25% Al shows no superdislocations due to low APB energies; single dislocations trail shear APB. No coupled four-partial dislocations were observed. Dipoles are present in all compositions, with superdislocation dipoles in ordered materials. High work hardening is linked to stronger dipole locks from superdislocations and screw dislocation tendency due to Peierls forces (not elastic anisotropy) for 25-30% Al. Dislocation reactions producing sessile <100> edges are seen near 28-30% Al, contributing to hardening. The ductility peak at Fe-28% Al is explained by high work hardening rate. No new materials, processing routes, or sample aliases are introduced. References section follows.
microstructure: paired superdislocations with straight screw segments (28-35% Al)
single dislocations trail shear APB (Fe-25% Al)
dipoles: single in disordered, superdislocation in ordered
properties: work hardening rate peak near Fe-28% Al
fall in flow stress above 25% Al
mechanisms: screw orientation from Peierls forces (25-30% Al)
dislocation reactions to sessile <100> edges (28-30% Al)
